debate.title: Internal Temperature of Stars

defs.def: Main sequence - The longest portion of a star's life, where it primarily fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
Equipartition theorem - In thermal equilibrium, energy is shared equally among all of its various forms.
First law of thermodynamics - Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.
Virial theorem - The average kinetic energy of the star is roughly equal to the negative half of its gravitational potential energy.
Thermal pressure - The outward pressure exerted by the random thermal motions of particles in a gas.

Stellar timescales - Stars function on three timescales, the amount of time required to exhaust its nuclear fuel, the amount of time required to radiate away all thermal energy as heat, and the amount of time required for material to free fall in the absence of all pressure. The nuclear timescale is the longest, followed by the thermal timescale and then the dynamical timescale.

Nuclear fusion - Stars fuse lighter nuclei into heavier nuclei to release immense amounts of energy during their lifetime. Hydrogen is fused into Helium at temperatures close to 10^7 (via the proton-proton chain) for the majority of a star's life.
Helium fusion - The nuclear process in which helium nuclei are fused into heavier elements (primarily carbon and oxygen), requiring core temperatures of roughly 10^8 Kelvin.
Kinetic energy - The energy possessed by particles due to their motion.
Gravitational potential energy - The energy stored in a system due to the gravitational attraction between its parts.

Photons - Massless quanta of electromagnetic radiation that carry energy and momentum. Stars emit photons across the electromagnetic spectrum, and the photons that escape a star's surface carry energy away from it.

debate.question:
How does the internal temperature of a star evolve with time on the main sequence?
The internal temperature of a star increases with time on the main sequence. A star continually radiates energy away from its surface in the form of photons.
The temperature of the system does not increase due to the amount of energy actively being taken away from it. The temperature of a star is proportional to the kinetic energy of its constituent particles via the equipartition theorem.
The kinetic energy of the constituent particles of the star increases, as it evolves, on the main sequence. By the first law of thermodynamics, energy lost via radiation must come from the system's internal energy or work done on the system.
Since no external work is being done on the system, the internal energy must decrease, which comes from the kinetic energy of the constituent particles. When a self-gravitating system contracts, its gravitational potential energy decreases.
Gravity does work on the system and the released gravitational potential energy more than offsets the radiated energy. The surplus increases the kinetic energy of the particles. Thermodynamic properties, which include internal energy, are intrinsically linked to the star's temperature.
Kinetic energy maps onto temperature via the equipartition theorem. In contrast, gravitational potential energy has no place in thermodynamic calculations. By the virial theorem, the kinetic energy of the star increases over time, as the gravitational potential energy of the star itself decreases. And since the temperature is proportional to the kinetic energy (), the temperature, on the whole, naturally increases. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
The internal temperature of a star decreases with time on the main sequence. Star cores contract as they evolve on the main sequence.
Gravity gets stronger as the star contracts. If the internal temperature also decreases, the star would collapse at a thermal timescale. Nuclear fusion generates the radiation pressure and thermal pressure that support the star against gravitational collapse.
Decreasing outward pressure as the star runs out of nuclear fuel causes the star's contraction. This does not cause collapse. Nuclear fusion in the star's core converts hydrogen into helium during the main sequence.
This in turn reduces the number of particles and hence the thermal pressure in the core. If the temperature does not increase to compensate, hydrostatic equilibrium cannot be maintained. Over time, the core gradually gets denser as hydrogen is steadily converted into helium.
The higher density enables the core to withstand more inward pressure from the outer layers of the star. This is why temperature does not need to increase to maintain equilibrium. The self-gravity of an object increases as it grows denser at constant mass.
The core's own inward pull and the pressure from the outer layers of the star grow stronger with contraction; the temperature must in turn increase to counteract them and prevent collapse. A star contracts until it ignites helium fusion at the end of the main sequence. The rate at which thermal pressure decreases matters: the increasing density of the core () prolongs the contraction long enough for helium fusion to ignite, preventing collapse. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
